title: Making and Keeping Friends as an Adult
prompt: Talk about how friendships work in adult life. Describe how you have made friends as an adult, what makes a friendship last, and what challenges adults face when trying to build or maintain friendships. Compare this with friendships earlier in your life if you wish.

examples.title: Friends: Then and Now
examples.body:
I think making friends as an adult is much harder than when you are a child or a student. When I was at school, I made friends very easily because I saw the same people every day and we had a lot in common. But as an adult, it requires more effort.

I have made some of my adult friends through work. When you spend a lot of time with the same people, it is natural to get closer to some of them. I have also met friends through hobbies, like a language class I joined a few years ago. I think joining a group or a club is one of the best ways to meet new people as an adult.

The challenge with adult friendships is that everyone is busy. People have jobs, families, and many responsibilities, so it can be difficult to find time to meet. I think this is why some adult friendships slowly fade — not because people stop caring, but because life gets in the way.

I believe a friendship lasts when both people make an effort. It doesn't have to be a lot — even a message to check in, or meeting for coffee occasionally, can keep a friendship alive. Trust and honesty are also very important. A good friend is someone you can talk to about real problems, not just small talk.

I still have some friends from school that I am very close to, and I think those friendships are special because we have known each other for so long. But I also really value the friendships I have made as an adult, because we have actively chosen to be in each other's lives.

examples.title: The Slow Work of Adult Friendship
examples.body:
Adult friendship is something I find genuinely more interesting to think about than I expected to when I was younger, precisely because it's so much more effortful than it appeared to be from a distance.

When we're children and adolescents, proximity does an enormous amount of the work. You see the same people every day; shared boredom creates intimacy; the stakes of social interaction are lower because you have relatively few other demands. Friendships form almost by accident in that context. As an adult, that scaffolding disappears almost overnight. You have to be intentional in a way that can feel, frankly, a little awkward — reaching out to someone you've met only once or twice, suggesting plans you're genuinely not sure will be accepted, investing time and emotional energy in something that might not develop into much.

The friendships I value most as an adult have formed through sustained, repeated contact in a context that had nothing to do with friendship — a work project, a class, a running group. That repetition seems to be the critical ingredient. It gives people the chance to be seen in different modes, to establish small rituals, to accumulate the kind of shared experience that generates genuine closeness rather than just familiarity.

What I find most challenging is maintaining friendships during periods of significant life change. When people have children, relocate, or go through professional transitions, the rhythm of a friendship is disrupted, and without active effort on both sides, it can quietly dissolve. I don't think this happens because people stop caring — it's more that adult life has an unusually powerful gravitational pull towards the immediate, and friendships, unlike work deadlines or family obligations, rarely announce their own neglect.

I've become more deliberate about this over the years. I try to be the person who initiates — who sends the message, books the dinner, makes the call. It doesn't come naturally to me, but I've noticed that the friends I value most tend to reciprocate when you create the opening. And that reciprocity — that mutual willingness to put in the work — is probably the most reliable sign that a friendship will actually endure.
